[Indo-European](/wiki/Indo-European_languages "Indo-European languages")  * [Germanic](/wiki/Germanic_languages "Germanic languages")   + [West Germanic](/wiki/West_Germanic_languages "West Germanic languages")     - [High German](/wiki/High_German_languages "High German languages")       * [Standard German](/wiki/Standard_German "Standard German")         + **Austrian German (Austrian)**

[newspapers](https://www.google.com/search?&q=%22Austrian+German%22&tbs=bkt:s&tbm=bks) **·** [books](https://www.google.com/search?tbs=bks:1&q=%22Austrian+German%22+-wikipedia) **·** [scholar](https://scholar.google.com/scholar?q=%22Austrian+German%22) **·** [JSTOR](https://www.jstor.org/action/doBasicSearch?Query=%22Austrian+German%22&acc=on&wc=on) *(April 2020)* *([Learn how and when to remove this message](/wiki/Help:Maintenance_template_removal "Help:Maintenance template removal"))* |

(**ASG**),[[3]](#cite_note-3)[[4]](#cite_note-4) **Standard Austrian German**[[5]](#cite_note-:0-5) (*Österreichisches Standarddeutsch*), **Austrian High German**[[2]](#cite_note-eurotopics-2)[[6]](#cite_note-6) (*Österreichisches Hochdeutsch*), or simply just **Austrian** (*Österreichisch*), is the variety of [Standard German](/wiki/Standard_German "Standard German") written and spoken in [Austria](/wiki/Austria "Austria") and [South Tyrol](/wiki/South_Tyrol "South Tyrol").[[7]](#cite_note-7) It has the highest [sociolinguistic prestige](/wiki/Prestige_(sociolinguistics) "Prestige (sociolinguistics)") locally, as it is the variation used in the media and for other formal situations. In less formal situations, Austrians use [Bavarian](/wiki/Bavarian_language "Bavarian language") and [Alemannic dialects](/wiki/Alemannic_German "Alemannic German"), which are traditionally spoken but rarely written in Austria. It has been standardized with the publishing of the *[Österreichisches Wörterbuch](/wiki/%C3%96sterreichisches_W%C3%B6rterbuch "Österreichisches Wörterbuch")* in 1951.[[8]](#cite_note-8)[[9]](#cite_note-9)

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Variety of Standard German

Not to be confused with [Austro-Bavarian dialects](/wiki/Austro-Bavarian_dialects "Austro-Bavarian dialects").

**Austrian German**[[2]](#cite_note-eurotopics-2) ([German](/wiki/German_language "German language"): *Österreichisches Deutsch*), **Austrian Standard German** (**ASG**),[[3]](#cite_note-3)[[4]](#cite_note-4) **Standard Austrian German**[[5]](#cite_note-:0-5) (*Österreichisches Standarddeutsch*), **Austrian High German**[[2]](#cite_note-eurotopics-2)[[6]](#cite_note-6) (*Österreichisches Hochdeutsch*), or simply just **Austrian** (*Österreichisch*), is the variety of [Standard German](/wiki/Standard_German "Standard German") written and spoken in [Austria](/wiki/Austria "Austria") and [South Tyrol](/wiki/South_Tyrol "South Tyrol").[[7]](#cite_note-7) It has the highest [sociolinguistic prestige](/wiki/Prestige_(sociolinguistics) "Prestige (sociolinguistics)") locally, as it is the variation used in the media and for other formal situations. In less formal situations, Austrians use [Bavarian](/wiki/Bavarian_language "Bavarian language") and [Alemannic dialects](/wiki/Alemannic_German "Alemannic German"), which are traditionally spoken but rarely written in Austria. It has been standardized with the publishing of the *[Österreichisches Wörterbuch](/wiki/%C3%96sterreichisches_W%C3%B6rterbuch "Österreichisches Wörterbuch")* in 1951.[[8]](#cite_note-8)[[9]](#cite_note-9)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Austrian_German&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

Austrian German has its beginning in the mid-18th century, when Empress [Maria Theresa](/wiki/Maria_Theresa "Maria Theresa") and her son [Joseph II](/wiki/Joseph_II,_Holy_Roman_Emperor "Joseph II, Holy Roman Emperor") introduced [compulsory schooling](/wiki/Compulsory_education "Compulsory education") in 1774, and several reforms of administration in their multilingual [Habsburg Empire](/wiki/Habsburg_Empire "Habsburg Empire"). At the time, the written standard was *Oberdeutsche Schreibsprache* ([Upper German](/wiki/Upper_German "Upper German") written language), which was highly influenced by the [Bavarian](/wiki/Bavarian_language "Bavarian language") and [Alemannic dialects](/wiki/Alemannic_dialects "Alemannic dialects") of Austria. Another option was to create a new standard based on the Southern German dialects, as proposed by the linguist [Johann Siegmund Popowitsch](/wiki/Johann_Siegmund_Popowitsch "Johann Siegmund Popowitsch"). Instead they decided for pragmatic reasons to adopt the already-standardized [chancellery](/wiki/Saxon_Electoral_Chancellery "Saxon Electoral Chancellery") language of [Saxony](/wiki/Saxony "Saxony") (*Sächsische Kanzleisprache* or *Meißner Kanzleideutsch*), which was based on the administrative language of the non-Austrian area of [Meißen](/wiki/Mei%C3%9Fen "Meißen") and [Dresden](/wiki/Dresden "Dresden").
Austria High German (*Hochdeutsch in Österreich*, not to be confused with the Bavarian Austria German dialects) has the same geographic origin as the [Swiss High German](/wiki/Swiss_High_German "Swiss High German") (*Schweizer Hochdeutsch*, not to be confused with the Alemannic [Swiss German](/wiki/Swiss_German "Swiss German") dialects).

The process of introducing the new written standard was led by [Joseph von Sonnenfels](/wiki/Joseph_von_Sonnenfels "Joseph von Sonnenfels").

Since 1951, the standardized form of Austrian German for official governmental use and in schools has been defined by the *[Österreichisches Wörterbuch](/wiki/%C3%96sterreichisches_W%C3%B6rterbuch "Österreichisches Wörterbuch")* ("Austrian Dictionary"), published originally at the behest of the Austrian [Federal Ministry of Education, Arts and Culture](/wiki/Federal_Ministry_of_Education,_Arts_and_Culture "Federal Ministry of Education, Arts and Culture") (in the 1950s the "Unterrichtsministerium", under minister Felix Hurdes) with Verlag Jugend & Volk, then by the Österreichischer Bundesverlag.[[10]](#cite_note-10)

## Standard Austrian German

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Austrian_German&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Standard Austrian German")]

The German language is a [pluricentric language](/wiki/Pluricentric_language "Pluricentric language") and Austrian German is one of its [standardized forms](/wiki/Standard_language "Standard language"). The official Austrian dictionary, *[Österreichisches Wörterbuch](/wiki/%C3%96sterreichisches_W%C3%B6rterbuch "Österreichisches Wörterbuch")*, [prescribes](/wiki/Linguistic_prescription "Linguistic prescription") spelling rules that define the official language.[[5]](#cite_note-:0-5)

Austrian delegates participated in the international working group that drafted the [German spelling reform](/wiki/German_orthography_reform_of_1996#In_Austria "German orthography reform of 1996") of 1996, and several conferences leading up to the reform were hosted in Vienna at the invitation of the Austrian federal government. Austria adopted it as a signatory, along with Germany, Switzerland, and Liechtenstein, of an international memorandum of understanding (Wiener Absichtserklärung) signed in Vienna in 1996.

The [eszett](/wiki/Eszett "Eszett") (ß) is used in Austria and Germany but not in Switzerland.[[11]](#cite_note-11)[[12]](#cite_note-12) In Austria, it is usually only called "scharfes S" ("sharp s").[[13]](#cite_note-13)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b6/Oesterreichische_Schulschrift_1995%2C_2_-_Schraegschrift.jpg/250px-Oesterreichische_Schulschrift_1995%2C_2_-_Schraegschrift.jpg)](/wiki/File:Oesterreichische_Schulschrift_1995,_2_-_Schraegschrift.jpg)

*Schulschrift* (1995), an Austrian primary-school handwriting style

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d0/Fu%C3%9Fgeher-Schild.JPG/250px-Fu%C3%9Fgeher-Schild.JPG)](/wiki/File:Fu%C3%9Fgeher-Schild.JPG)

A sign in Vienna: *Fußgeher* ("pedestrian") is *Fußgänger* in Germany. In all-caps words, [capital ẞ](/wiki/Capital_%E1%BA%9E "Capital ẞ") (instead of *SS*) became standard in both nations in 2017, but *SS* remains valid.

Distinctions in vocabulary persist, for example, in [culinary](/wiki/Culinary "Culinary") terms, for which communication with Germans is frequently difficult, and [administrative](/wiki/Administration_(government) "Administration (government)") and [legal](/wiki/Law "Law") language because of Austria's exclusion from the development of a German [nation-state](/wiki/Nation-state "Nation-state") in the late 19th century and its manifold particular traditions. A comprehensive collection of Austrian-German legal, administrative and economic terms is offered in *Markhardt, Heidemarie: Wörterbuch der österreichischen Rechts-, Wirtschafts- und Verwaltungsterminologie* (Peter Lang, 2006).

Because of German's [pluricentric](/wiki/Pluricentric_language "Pluricentric language") nature, German dialects in Austria should not be confused with the variety of Standard Austrian German spoken by most Austrians, which is distinct from that of [Germany](/wiki/Germany "Germany") or [Switzerland](/wiki/Switzerland "Switzerland"). In the field of German dialectology, the notion of Standard Austrian German has been both debated and defended by German linguists since the 1970s. A [One Standard German Axiom](/wiki/One_Standard_German_Axiom "One Standard German Axiom"), effectively preventing the development of newer standards of German, has recently been offered as a characteristic of the field but remains to be discussed discipline-internally.[[14]](#cite_note-14)

### Former spoken standard

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Austrian_German&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Former spoken standard")]

Until 1918, the spoken standard in Austria was the *Schönbrunner Deutsch*, a sociolect spoken by the imperial [Habsburg family](/wiki/Habsburg_family "Habsburg family") and the nobility of [Austria-Hungary](/wiki/Austria-Hungary "Austria-Hungary"). The sociolect, a variety of Standard German, is influenced by [Viennese German](/wiki/Viennese_German "Viennese German") and other Austro-Bavarian dialects spoken in eastern Austria but is slightly [nasalized](/wiki/Nasalization "Nasalization").[[15]](#cite_note-15)[[16]](#cite_note-16)[[note 1]](#cite_note-17)

### Special written forms

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Austrian_German&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Special written forms")]

For many years, Austria had a special form of the language for official government documents that is known as *Österreichische Kanzleisprache*, or "[Austrian chancellery](/wiki/Austrian_chancellery "Austrian chancellery") language". It is a very traditional form of the language, probably derived from medieval [deeds](/wiki/Deed "Deed") and documents, and has a very complex structure and vocabulary generally reserved for such documents. For most speakers (even native speakers), this form of the language is generally difficult to understand, as it contains many highly specialised terms for diplomatic, internal, official, and military matters. There are no regional variations because the special written form has been used mainly by a government that has now, for centuries, been based in Vienna.

*Österreichische Kanzleisprache* is now used less and less because of various administrative reforms that reduced the number of traditional civil servants (*[Beamte](/wiki/Beamter "Beamter")*). As a result, [Standard Austrian German](/wiki/Standard_Austrian_German "Standard Austrian German") is replacing it in government and administrative texts.

### European Union

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Austrian_German&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: European Union")]

When Austria became a member of the [European Union](/wiki/European_Union "European Union") on 1 January 1995, 23 food-related terms were listed in its [accession](/wiki/Treaty_of_Accession_1994 "Treaty of Accession 1994") agreement as having the same legal status as the equivalent terms used in Germany,[[17]](#cite_note-18)
for example, the words for "potato", "tomato", and "Brussels sprouts".[[note 2]](#cite_note-20) (Examples in "[Vocabulary](#Vocab_difference)")
Austrian German is the only variety of a [pluricentric language](/wiki/Pluricentric_language "Pluricentric language") recognized under international law or EU primary law.[[19]](#cite_note-21) The focus on food-related vocabulary in "Protocol 23" is owed to trade requirements and therefore utterly accidental.[[20]](#cite_note-22)

### Grammar

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Austrian_German&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Grammar")]

#### Verbs

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Austrian_German&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Verbs")]

In [Austria](/wiki/Austria "Austria"), as in the [German-speaking parts of Switzerland](/wiki/Languages_of_Switzerland#German "Languages of Switzerland") and in [southern Germany](/wiki/Southern_Germany "Southern Germany"), verbs that express a state tend to use *sein* as the [auxiliary verb](/wiki/Auxiliary_verb "Auxiliary verb") in the [perfect](/wiki/Perfect_(grammar) "Perfect (grammar)"), as well as verbs of movement. [Verbs](/wiki/German_verb "German verb") which fall into this category include *sitzen* (to sit), *liegen* (to lie) and, in parts of [Styria](/wiki/Styria "Styria") and [Carinthia](/wiki/Carinthia_(state) "Carinthia (state)"), *schlafen* (to sleep). Therefore, the perfect of these verbs would be *ich bin gesessen*, *ich bin gelegen* and *ich bin geschlafen*, respectively.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

In Germany, the words *stehen* (to stand) and *gestehen* (to confess) are identical in the present perfect: *habe gestanden*. The Austrian variant avoids that potential ambiguity (*bin gestanden* from *stehen*, "to stand"; and *habe gestanden* from *gestehen*, "to confess": *"der Verbrecher ist vor dem Richter gestanden und hat gestanden"*).[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

In addition, the [preterite](/wiki/Preterite "Preterite") ([simple past](/wiki/Simple_past "Simple past")) is very rarely used in Austria, especially in the [spoken language](/wiki/Spoken_language "Spoken language"), with the exception of some [modal verbs](/wiki/Modal_verb "Modal verb") (*ich sollte*, *ich wollte*).[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

### Vocabulary

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Austrian_German&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Vocabulary")]

There are many official terms that differ in Austrian German from their usage in most parts of [Germany](/wiki/Germany "Germany"). Words used in Austria are *Jänner* (January) rather than *Januar*,[[21]](#cite_note-23) *Feber* (more rare than *Jänner*) in variation with *Februar*, *heuer* (this year) along with *dieses Jahr*, *Stiege* (stairs) along with *Treppen*, *Rauchfang* (chimney) instead of *Schornstein*, many administrative, legal and political terms, and many food terms, including the following:[[22]](#cite_note-24)[[23]](#cite_note-25)

| Austrian Standard German | Standard German | English |
| --- | --- | --- |
| *Brandteigkrapferl* | *Windbeutel* | Cream puff |
| *Eierspeise* | *Rühreier* | Scrambled eggs |
| *Erdapfel (*also Bavarian and Southern German) | *Kartoffel* | Potato |
| *Faschiertes* | *Hackfleisch* | Minced meat/Ground beef |
| *Fisolen* | *Gartenbohnen*  or *Grüne Bohnen* | Common beans/green beans |
| *Karfiol* (also Bavarian and Southern German) | *Blumenkohl* | Cauliflower |
| *Kohlsprossen* | *Rosenkohl* | Brussel sprouts |
| *Kren* (also Bavarian and Southern German) | *Meerrettich* | Horseradish |
| *Kukuruz* (southeastern and western Austria) | *Mais* | Maize/corn |
| *Marille* | *Aprikose* | Apricot |
| *Melange* | *Milchkaffee* | Milk heavy coffee drink |
| *Melanzani* | *Aubergine* | Aubergine/eggplant |
| *Palatschinke* | *Pfannkuchen* | Pancake |
| *Paradeiser*  (Vienna, Eastern Austria) | *Tomate* | Tomato |
| *Pfefferoni* | *Peperoni* or *Chili* | Chili pepper |
| *Rote Rübe* | *Rote Bete* | Beetroot |
| *Sauce Tartare* | *Remoulade* | Tartar Sauce |
| *Schlagobers* | *Schlagsahne* | Whipped cream |
| *Stanitzel* | *Eiswaffel* | Ice cream cone |
| *Staubzucker* | *Puderzucker* | Icing sugar/powdered sugar |
| *Topfen* (also Bavarian) | *Quark* | [Quark](/wiki/Quark_(dairy_product) "Quark (dairy product)"), a semi-sweet cottage cheese |
| *Weckerl* (also Bavarian) | *Brötchen* | Roll (bread) |

There are, however, some [false friends](/wiki/False_friends "False friends") between the two regional varieties:

* *Kasten* (wardrobe) along with or instead of *Schrank* as opposed to *Kiste* (box) instead of *Kasten*. *Kiste* in Germany means both "box" and "chest". Similarly, *Eiskasten* along with *Kühlschrank* (refrigerator).
* *Sessel* (chair) instead of *Stuhl*. *Sessel* means "[easy chair](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/easy_chair "wikt:easy chair")" in Germany and *Stuhl* means "stool (faeces)" in both varieties.

## Dialects

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Austrian_German&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Dialects")]

### Classification

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Austrian_German&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Classification")]

* Dialects of the [Austro-Bavarian group](/wiki/Austro-Bavarian_language "Austro-Bavarian language"), which also comprises dialects from [Bavaria](/wiki/Bavaria "Bavaria")
  + [Central Austro-Bavarian](/wiki/Central_Austro-Bavarian "Central Austro-Bavarian") (along the main rivers [Isar](/wiki/Isar "Isar") and [Danube](/wiki/Danube "Danube"), spoken in the northern parts of the [State of Salzburg](/wiki/Salzburg_(state) "Salzburg (state)"), [Upper Austria](/wiki/Upper_Austria "Upper Austria"), [Lower Austria](/wiki/Lower_Austria "Lower Austria"), and northern [Burgenland](/wiki/Burgenland "Burgenland"))
    - [Viennese German](/wiki/Viennese_German "Viennese German")
  + [Southern Austro-Bavarian](/wiki/Southern_Austro-Bavarian "Southern Austro-Bavarian") (in [Tyrol](/wiki/Tyrol_(state) "Tyrol (state)"), [South Tyrol](/wiki/South_Tyrol "South Tyrol"), [Carinthia](/wiki/Carinthia_(state) "Carinthia (state)"), [Styria](/wiki/Styria "Styria"), and the southern parts of Salzburg and Burgenland)
* [Vorarlbergerisch](/wiki/Vorarlbergerisch "Vorarlbergerisch"), spoken in [Vorarlberg](/wiki/Vorarlberg "Vorarlberg"), is a [High Alemannic](/wiki/High_Alemannic "High Alemannic") dialect.

### Regional accents

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Austrian_German&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Regional accents")]

In addition to the standard variety, in everyday life most Austrians speak one of a number of [Upper German dialects](/wiki/Upper_German_dialects "Upper German dialects").[[24]](#cite_note-26)

While strong forms of the various dialects are not fully [mutually intelligible](/wiki/Mutually_intelligible "Mutually intelligible") to [northern Germans](/wiki/Northern_Germany "Northern Germany"), communication is much easier in [Bavaria](/wiki/Bavaria "Bavaria"), especially rural areas, where the Bavarian dialect still predominates as the mother tongue. The [Central Austro-Bavarian](/wiki/Central_Austro-Bavarian "Central Austro-Bavarian") dialects are more intelligible to speakers of [Standard German](/wiki/Standard_German "Standard German") than the [Southern Austro-Bavarian](/wiki/Southern_Austro-Bavarian "Southern Austro-Bavarian") dialects of [Tyrol](/wiki/Tyrol_(state) "Tyrol (state)").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

[Viennese](/wiki/Viennese_language "Viennese language"), the Austro-Bavarian dialect of [Vienna](/wiki/Vienna "Vienna"), is seen by many in Germany as quintessentially Austrian. The people of [Graz](/wiki/Graz "Graz"), the capital of [Styria](/wiki/Styria "Styria"), speak yet another dialect which is not very Styrian and more easily understood by people from other parts of Austria than other Styrian dialects, for example, from western Styria.

Simple words in the various dialects are very similar, but pronunciation is distinct for each and, after listening to a few spoken words, it may be possible for an Austrian to realise which [dialect](/wiki/Dialect "Dialect") is being spoken. However, in regard to the dialects of the deeper valleys of the Tyrol, other Tyroleans are often unable to understand them. Speakers from the different [provinces of Austria](/wiki/Provinces_of_Austria "Provinces of Austria") can easily be distinguished from each other by their particular accents (probably more so than Bavarians), those of [Carinthia](/wiki/Carinthia_(state) "Carinthia (state)"), Styria, Vienna, [Upper Austria](/wiki/Upper_Austria "Upper Austria"), and the Tyrol being very characteristic. Speakers from those regions, even those speaking Standard German, can usually be easily identified by their accent, even by an untrained listener.

Several of the dialects have been influenced by contact with non-Germanic linguistic groups, such as the dialect of Carinthia, where, in the past, many speakers were bilingual (and, in the southeastern portions of the state, many still are even today) with [Slovene](/wiki/Slovene_language "Slovene language"), and the dialect of Vienna, which has been influenced by immigration during the [Austro-Hungarian](/wiki/Austria-Hungary "Austria-Hungary") period, particularly from what is today the [Czech Republic](/wiki/Czech_Republic "Czech Republic"). The German dialects of [South Tyrol](/wiki/South_Tyrol "South Tyrol") have been influenced by local [Romance languages](/wiki/Romance_languages "Romance languages"), particularly noticeable with the many [loanwords](/wiki/Loanword "Loanword") from [Italian](/wiki/Italian_language "Italian language") and [Ladin](/wiki/Ladin_language "Ladin language").

The geographic borderlines between the different accents ([isoglosses](/wiki/Isogloss "Isogloss")) coincide strongly with the borders of the states and also with the border with Bavaria, with Bavarians having a markedly different rhythm of speech in spite of the linguistic similarities.
